content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN consisting of **positive** integers.

In one move, Chef can do the following:


Pick an index i (1‚â§i‚â§N)i \ (1 \leq i \leq N)i (1‚â§i‚â§N) and a prime number PPP, such that PPP divides AiA_iAi‚Äã.
Divide AiA_iAi‚Äã by PPP, i.e, set Ai:=AiPA_i := \frac{A_i}{P}Ai‚Äã:=PAi‚Äã‚Äã.
Find the **minimum** number of moves that Chef needs to make the array AAA **non-decreasing**.

Note: An array AAA is called non-decreasing if Ai‚â§A(i+1)A_i \le A_{(i+1)}Ai‚Äã‚â§A(i+1)‚Äã for all (1‚â§i<N)(1 \le i \lt N)(1‚â§i<N).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, the length of array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.




Output Format

For each test case, output on a new line the **minimum** number of moves needed to make AAA non-decreasing.
constraints: The sum of NNN across all test cases won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef can perform the following moves:

Divide A1=3A_1 = 3A1‚Äã=3 by P=3P = 3P=3. Now, A=[1,2,1]A = [1, 2, 1]A=[1,2,1]
Divide A2=2A_2 = 2A2‚Äã=2 by P=2P = 2P=2. Now, A=[1,1,1]A = [1, 1, 1]A=[1,1,1] and is non-decreasing.

Test case 222: One optimal final array is A=[1,1,1,2,2]A = [1, 1, 1, 2, 2]A=[1,1,1,2,2]. You can verify that it takes 999 moves to reach this:

222 moves each on positions 1,3,1, 3,1,3, and 444.
333 moves on position 222.

Test case 333: No moves need to be made. The array is already non-decreasing.
Test case 444: Chef can divide A1A_1A1‚Äã by 555 and A2A_2A2‚Äã by 333. The array is now [2,3,5,6,7,8][2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8][2,3,5,6,7,8], which is non-decreasing.
